coredata.dc:title: Smart “on-off-on” fluorescent switches for drug visual loading and responsive delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Peiwei
Zhang, Lei
Peng, Jingyi
Li, Shuohan
Chen, Junfeng
Liu, Xicheng
Peng, Hongwei
Liu, Zhe
You, Jinmao
coredata.dc:description: The unsatisfactory therapeutic effects and limitations of chemotherapy are urging people to find more effective therapy methods, and drug delivery systems have been attracting increasing research interests. However, most of these studies mainly focused on the therapeutic effects, while little attention was paid to explore new drug loading patterns and improve the efficiency. Herein, a pair of smart “on-off-on” fluorescent switches based on a novel double fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system was established to realize facilely visual drug loading and responsive delivery. To build this nanosystem, highly luminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were first prepared and further loaded by anticancer drugs of doxorubicin (DOX). The π-π and hydrogen-bonding interactions between them readily formed the first FRET system, which allowed to facilely monitor the drug loading process by detecting the solution color changes with naked eyes. Then, manganese dioxide (MnO2) with high surface area, further modified by polydopamine and folic acid was synthesized and served as the other FRET receptor for CQDs and DOX, facilitating monitor of full drug absorption and loading by total fluorescence quenching. Moreover, when the double-FRET nanosystem was endocytosed into cancer cells, MnO2 was responsively degraded into Mn2+ under intracellular glutathione and acid conditions, which resulted into sustained drug release and gradual fluorescence recovery. More importantly, the constructed nano-system also exhibited selectively drug delivery and showed much finer therapeutic effects than single chemotherapy, holding great potential in future cancer therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Carbon quantum dots
Visual loading
Drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of MnO2 nanoparticles
Cytotoxicity assay and cancer treatment evaluation
Structure, chemical composition and properties of MnO2 and CQDs
Visual loading of DOX onto CQDs under a UV lamp
Visual loading of CQDs + DOX onto MnO2-PDA-FA
Cell uptake and drug release in cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation ( ZR2018QB002 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51905304 , 21671118 , 31901188 ), Higher Educational Science and Technology Program ( J16LA03 ) of Shandong, and the Taishan Scholars. We also thanks ths financial support from National Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship ( 201910446027 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Novel double FRET nano drug loading system: MnO2@CQDs synthetic route, drug targeted delivery and dual trigger responsive drug release process.

(a) TEM image and (b) XRD data for MnO2; (c) XPS survey spectrum for MnO2-PDA-FA; (d) TEM image of CQDs and fluorescent image under 365 nm excitation; (e) PL performance of CQDs under different excitation wavelengths; (f) PL and PLE spectra of CQDs.

(a) FTIR data for DOX, CQDs and CQDs + DOX; (b) PL intensity changes of DOX with increased CQDs concentrations; (c) visual color changes of CQDs solutions with different drug loading contents. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) FTIR data for MnO2-PDA-FA, MnO2-PDA-FA + CQDs + DOX; (b) TEM image of MnO2-PDA-FA + CQDs + DOX, and the inset show the HRTEM images of CQDs loaded onto MnO2; (c) visual color changes of CQDs + DOX solutions with different MnO2 contents. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Changes of fluorescence of MnO2-PDA-FA + CQDs + DOX by adding various reactants (1 mM, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Gly, His, Asp, Lys, Val, Asn, Pro, Leu, Cys, DTT, GSH); (b) Fluorescence recovery data of DOX upon adding different amounts of GSH; (c) Relationship between fluorescence recovery intensity and GSH amounts.

Flow cytometry data to study the cell uptake and targeting ability. (a) HeLa and A549 cells treated by MnO2-PDA-FA + DOX under the same conditions; (b) Results of several independent experiments under the same conditions.
Laser scanning confocal microscope images for cells incubated with MnO2-PDA-FA + DOX with concentrations of (a) 2.5 μg/mL and (b) 10 μg/mL for 1 h.

(a) Cell viability for MnO2 and MnO2-PDA-FA; (b) HeLa cancer cells and A549 cancer cells incubated with MnO2-PDA-FA + CQDs + DOX for 24 h; HeLa cells with DOX, MnO2-PDA + CQDs + DOX and MnO2-PDA-FA + CQDs + DOX for (c) 24 h and 48 h.

head.ce:title: Smart “on-off-on” fluorescent switches for drug visual loading and responsive delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Plateau Ecology and Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Plateau Ecology and Agriculture, Qinghai University, Xining, 810016, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The unsatisfactory therapeutic effects and limitations of chemotherapy are urging people to find more effective therapy methods, and drug delivery systems have been attracting increasing research interests. However, most of these studies mainly focused on the therapeutic effects, while little attention was paid to explore new drug loading patterns and improve the efficiency. Herein, a pair of smart “on-off-on” fluorescent switches based on a novel double fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system was established to realize facilely visual drug loading and responsive delivery. To build this nanosystem, highly luminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were first prepared and further loaded by anticancer drugs of doxorubicin (DOX). The π-π and hydrogen-bonding interactions between them readily formed the first FRET system, which allowed to facilely monitor the drug loading process by detecting the solution color changes with naked eyes. Then, manganese dioxide (MnO2) with high surface area, further modified by polydopamine and folic acid was synthesized and served as the other FRET receptor for CQDs and DOX, facilitating monitor of full drug absorption and loading by total fluorescence quenching. Moreover, when the double-FRET nanosystem was endocytosed into cancer cells, MnO2 was responsively degraded into Mn2+ under intracellular glutathione and acid conditions, which resulted into sustained drug release and gradual fluorescence recovery. More importantly, the constructed nano-system also exhibited selectively drug delivery and showed much finer therapeutic effects than single chemotherapy, holding great potential in future cancer therapy.

A pair of smart “on-off-on” fluorescent switches based on a novel double fluorescence resonance energy transfer system was established to realize facilely visual drug loading and responsive delivery.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: As the most principal cause of death worldwide, cancer become a worldwide problem that heavily threatens human life and health [1–3]. Among many of the cancer therapy means, chemotherapy still serves as the most generally used one, although its therapeutic effect is far from satisfaction due to poor efficacy resulting from insufficient drug doses in tumor sites, increasing toxic side effects because of nonspecific anticancer drug delivery, and drug-resistance as a result of long-term mono-therapy, etc. [4–8] In order to achieve better therapeutic effect, it is necessary to improve drug targetability toward cancer cells, increase drug dose and prolong drug release time in cancer cells. Characteristic of facile surface modification, easy cell uptake, high drug loading contents, and controlled drug release behavior, smart and responsive drug delivery systems (DDS) that employ nanomaterials with multiple function as drug nanocarriers are attracting more and more attention and research interest [9–13].

As a new type of nanomaterials, nanosized manganese dioxide (MnO2) not only has the advantages of large surface area, high drug loading contents and small cytotoxicity, but also is expected to become a new concept of DDS due to many significant advantages distinguished from other nanomaterials: (1) Facile synthesis and well control over structure and chemistry. Most synthesis methods of MnO2 process were simple, and its size as well as surface could be readily controlled by adjusting the reaction time and reagents [14]. (2) Good biocompatibility and easy surface modification. Besides manganese is an essential trace element in the human body, MnO2 didn't exhibit obvious toxicity toward cells and living body [15,16]. Meanwhile, its good water dispersibility allowed various surface functionalization by facile chemical and physical strategy, which further endowed MnO2 better biocompatibility and special selectivity toward cancer cells [17,18]. (3) Smart responsiveness toward GSH or H+/H2O2. Many tumor sites possessed high GSH contents and lower pH value, both of which could induce the decomposition of MnO2 into Mn2+, while the latter could be easily excreted out of human body by many metabolic pathways, making it a good biodegradable nanocarrier [19–22]. (4) Effective contrast for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and fluorescence quenching agent. When fluorescent drugs or biomarkers were loaded onto MnO2, their fluorescence could be effectively quenched due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect. And when MnO2 was decomposed into Mn2+, the fluorescence could be restored and Mn2+ could serve as T1 contrast agent for MRI [23–28], both of which making it an effective fluorescent probe for drug delivery and cell imaging [29–31]. These unique properties of MnO2 make it a promising novel material for smart DDS and MRI bio-labeling agent to track the intracellular drug distribution and therapy effect.

On the other hand, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) that possessed narrow luminescent wave range, high quantum yield, long fluorescence life, long circulation time in vivo and good passive targeting effect on tumor etc., have become a hot spot in the field of transmitting mediated chemotherapy drugs to tumor sites for imaging and treatment of tumors [32–36]. In addition, due to the aromatic conjugated structure, CQDs can also be co-located with drugs for chemotherapy [37–40], which facilitates real-time monitoring and imaging guided treatment. Accordingly, most of the reported works were focused on the synthesis and application of CQDs for bio-imaging and drug delivery, yet few studies were carried out to investigate the possibility of CQDs that severs as a novel material to monitor the drug loading process, especially to detect the drug loading behavior under naked eyes instead of employing analytical instrument.

Here, a novel strategy that combined the excellent properties of MnO2 and CQDs and realized visual detection of drug loading, targeted drug delivery, responsive biodegradation/drug release, and effective cancer chemotherapy was developed. To construct this multifunctional system, a highly fluorescent N-doped CQDs with quantum yield of 45% was first synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method, and anticancer drugs of doxorubicin (DOX) was loaded onto CQDs by π-π interactions between them. This design resulted into an effective FRET system, and the fluorescence intensity and solution-color gradually changed with different amounts of DOX being absorbed onto CQDs, which finally allowed visual detection of drug loading process under naked eyes. Meanwhile, nanosized flower-like MnO2 with high surface area was then prepared and further modified with polydopamine (PDA) and folic acid (FA), rendering good dispersibility in various media and special selectivity toward cancer cells that overexpressed FA receptors. Moreover, the drug-enriched CQDs + DOX was further loaded onto MnO2-PDA-FA by the van der Waals interactions between them, and this allowed us to build the other effective FRET system, which the fluorescence of CQDs + DOX could be totally quenched by adjusting the loading contents. After selectively uptaken into cancer cells, MnO2 was responsively degraded into Mn2+ under intracellular GSH conditions and this then induced responsive drug release, which could be monitored by detecting the fluorescence recovery of CQDs or DOX and to investigate the drug distribution and therapy effects. These results were systematacially investigated by various chemical, structural, analytical and cell experiments, and the constructed MnO2-PDA-FA + CQDs + DOX system exhibited good biocompatibility, selectivity, and cancer therapy effects (see Fig. 1). The novel methods and interesting experiment results would shed new light for designing and developing multifunctional DDS that could combine visual drug loading, effective targeted delivery, responsive release, and good chemotherapy effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of MnO2 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, MnO2 nanoparticals were prepared by oleic acid template method with some modifications [41]. The improved synthesis method was simple and feasible, and the size of the synthesized MnO2 could be well controlled within a size range that was easily taken up by cells. Details of MnO2 synthesis were provided in section 1.1 of supporting information.

For more information on surface modification of MnO2, please refer to section 1.2 of the support information. In order to achieve targeted transport of the drug, which is mainly concentrated in the lesion during drug administration, we decided to modify MnO2 with specific targeted ligands that can identify tumor cells. In the process of modification, we used the self-crosslinking property of dopamine in weak alkali water to form a polyurethane layer on the MnO2's surface. Through the amidation reaction between –NH2 groups of dopamine and –COOH groups of FA, targeted molecular of FA was grafted onto the surface of MnO2. The change in the composition of modified carrier was confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and FTIR spectrum.

Next, we used a one-pot method to synthesize CQDs using hydroquinone and ethylenediamine as raw materials based on our previous work with some modifications [42], and performed optimal excitation wavelength test. Parts 1.3 and 1.4 in the supporting information provided sample preparation and measurements.

We loaded MnO2 with DOX to measure its drug loading contents. The optimal loading ratio between DOX and CQDs and the effect of DOX with different amounts on the change of fluorescence intensity in CQDs were investigated by PL spectrum, naked eye and ultraviolet lamp observation. We also investigated the quenching of fluorescence in CQDs + DOX solution under naked eye and ultraviolet lamp after adding a series of different amounts of MnO2. Details of drug loading test and visual loading were provided in sections 1.5 and 1.6 of the support information section.

Different concentrations of glutathione (GSH) were added to MnO2-PDA-FA + CQDs + DOX and the whole process of drug release was monitored by fluorescence spectrophotometer. Detail of drug-controlled release was provided in section 1.7 of the support information section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assay and cancer treatment evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental details for sample preparation and cell evaluation of the chemotherapy were provided in Part 1.8 in the supporting information part.
Part 1.9 of the supporting information section provided characterization details of chemical composition, structure, PL, visual drug loading, cell uptake, and cancer treatment, etc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure, chemical composition and properties of MnO2 and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As showed in Fig. 2 a, nanosized MnO2 with uniform flower-like structure that consisted with thin and transparent sheets were readily obtained, and the average size of MnO2 was about 30 nm, a size that greatly facilitated further cellular uptake by endocytosis. It also has the advantage of large specific surface area (Fig. S1). X-ray diffraction patterns in Fig. 2b indicated typical δ type crystal structure of MnO2, which possessed characteristic peaks located at 2θ = 12.2°, 25°and 37.3°, which corresponded to (002), (200) and (101) diffraction patterns, respectively [43]. Pure MnO2 only possessed Mn and O with element contents ratio around 1:2, while after being modified by PDA and FA on the surface, obvious C and N elements were detected by XPS (Fig. 2c), suggesting effective surface modification. And this was also validated by FTIR study in Fig. S2. Transmission electron microscope image in Fig. 2d showed that CQDs with an average size of 2.5 nm was obtained (Fig. S3). FTIR data (Fig. 3 a) showed that typical C–O bonds (1084 cm−1), C=O bonds (1660 cm−1), C–N (1240 cm−1) and C–N = stretch bands (1315 cm−1) were detected from CQDs [44]. The uniform structure of CQDs guaranteed a high quantum yield of about 45%, and exhibited bright fluorescence under 365 nm excitation (inset in Fig. 2d). Detailed PL investigation in Fig. 2e indicated that the emission peaks of CQDs were almost unchanged under different excitation wavelengths. PL and PLE curves in Fig. 2f showed that emission peak of CQDs was around 520 nm, while the excitation peak was located at 405 nm. These results demonstrated that our methods were very facile and effective to synthesize nano-MnO2 and CQDs enriching both oxygen and nitrogen groups to enjoy good water-solubility (Fig. S4) and excellent PL performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual loading of DOX onto CQDs under a UV lamp
ce:section.ce:para: The aromatic structure and high water-solubility made CQDs very valuable for loading drugs, and in this work, DOX, a broad-spectrum and fluorescent anticancer drug for cancer therapy (the PL curves of DOX were provided in Fig. S5), was chosen to be loaded onto CQDs by π-π interactions between them. The successful drug loading process was first verified by FTIR data. As shown in Fig. 3a, the characteristic peaks of DOX were located at 1615 and 1583 cm−1 [45], corresponding to N–H groups in the drug. These typical peaks were also readily found in CQDs + DOX samples. The drug loading process was also confirmed by PL experiment. DOX possessed PL performance in water, and when the drug was loaded onto the surface of CQDs, a FRET system was constructed. It was speculated that when more and more DOX were absorbed onto the surface of CQDs, the fluorescence intensity of DOX gradually decreased. And when the absorption process was saturated and reached a balance, the fluorescence intensity should unchanged and accordingly the surface of CQDs was fully covered by DOX. To verify this hypothesis, different amounts of CQDs (from 13.5 to 162 μg/mL) were gradually added into DOX solutions, and corresponding data were provided in Fig. 3b. It was found that with more and more CQDs being added, the fluorescence intensity of DOX gradually decreased and then the ratio of mass concentration of CQDs to DOX reached 11.25 : 1, the fluorescence intensity almost unchanged, indicating the absorption process was saturated. More importantly, if we changed the order of adding CQDs into DOX solution, it was also observed that with different amounts of DOX being added, the color of CQDs under UV lamp gradually changed, and when the color of the solution changed from teal to orange, the absorption process reached balance and no more drugs could be further loaded. Therefore, the loading amount of DOX onto CQDs can be monitored by observing the changes of solution color with naked eye under an ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 3c). Compared with common methods to monitor the drug loading process using spectroscopic instruments like ultraviolet spectrophotometer, our visual loading method can greatly simplify experimental process and improve efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual loading of CQDs + DOX onto MnO2-PDA-FA
ce:section.ce:para: The successful loading CQDs + DOX onto MnO2 was directly reflected by TEM (Fig. 4 a), in which CQDs in the form of black dots were observed on the thin sheets of MnO2. HR TEM images showed that these black dots possessed typical crystal graphic lattice constant of 0.31 nm [37]. Element analyses from FTIR data in Fig. 4b also confirmed this result, in which the characteristic peaks of DOX at 1622 cm−1 and CQDs at 1647 cm−1 were detected in MnO2-PDA-FA + CQDs + DOX samples. It was reported that the high fluorescence of CQDs was attributed to radiative recombination of electrons as well as hole confinement over the surface [46]. And when CQDs were loaded onto other materials, the electrons and holes were often trapped, which made it less effective for radiative recombination and finally resulted into PL quenching by the FRET mechanism. In this work, it was found that not only the PL of CQDs could be well quenched by the MnO2 (Fig. S6a), but also the PL of CQDs + DOX could be quenched by MnO2 (Fig. S6b). This founding then allowed us to construct the other FRET system to monitor the loading process just by detecting the PL changes of CQDs + DOX, whose PL intensity gradually decreased with more and more MnO2 added. It was found that when the ratio of CQDs + DOX to MnO2 reached 1:33.3, the fluorescence was totally quenched, indicating the surface of MnO2 was fully covered by CQDs + DOX. This process can be visually monitored by naked eyes under both natural and UV light (Fig. 4c), which also avoids complex instrumental detection and shows greater simplicity and convenience.

Next, we further studied the controlled release of MnO2-PDA-FA + CQDs + DOX system. When MnO2 decomposed, the FRET system was destroyed and the fluorescence gradually recovered, which further suggested that CQDs + DOX were released from MnO2. To study the responsive decomposition property, various inorganic salt and amino acids that existed in cells were tested and analyzed (Fig. 5 a). It was concluded that decomposition of MnO2 was much more sensitive toward GSH, whose reduction and decomposition efficiency was much higher than other substances. When MnO2-PDA-FA + CQDs + DOX drug delivery system is ingested by cancer cells, MnO2 reacts with GSH to produce oxidative glutathione and Mn2+ (MnO2+GSH→Mn2++GSSG) [47]. Meanwhile, it has been reported that the contents of GSH in cancer cells were much higher than that in normal cells, and thus responsive decomposition and drug release could be readily achieved. Moreover, in order to further verify the effect of GSH on the reductive decomposition of MnO2, different amounts of GSH were added, and it was found that with GSH increasing, the fluorescence intensity of DOX gradually recovered and gained intensity (Fig. 5b–c). On the other hand, it was reported that MnO2 could decompose in an acidic environment, and it was well known that pH value in tumor microenvironment (TME) was less than 6. Therefore, when ultra-small nanoparticles MnO2+CQDs + DOX were untaken into cancer cells, acid-triggered degradation would also occur and accordingly DOX would release. This effective and ingenious dual-triggered and controlled release in TME greatly reduced the release of the drug in the transport process, decreased the side-effect toward normal cells and improved the efficacy of the drug. Moreover, compared with the common methods that using UV–vis spectrophotometer to monitor the drug loading and release process, this responsive and switchable fluorescence property also endowed us to innovatively construct a visual and color way to keep track of the drug loading and release process.

Good control over the size and surface of MnO2 then greatly facilitated targeted cell uptake of this nanocarrier into cells, and this was first confirmed by flow cytometry experiments (Fig. 6 a). Due to the high fluorescence property of DOX, no additional fluorescence labeling was needed to detect the cell uptake and intracellular drug release process. It was found that compared with the control cells, both A549 and HeLa cells exhibited obvious fluorescence signal, revealing that MnO2-PDA-FA + DOX was effectively endocytosed by cells and DOX was readily released. Moreover, the fluorescence signal of DOX in HeLa cells with FA receptors was much higher than that of A549 cells without FA receptors [48,49]. And three independent experiments confirmed that a high value of about 2 times larger was obtained (Fig. 6b), demonstrating the effective targeting drug delivery of MnO2 after FA modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell uptake and drug release in cells
ce:section.ce:para: Then, the cell uptake and drug release performance were further and visually monitored by laser scanning confocal microscope in Fig. 7 . It was observed that the green signal of DOX was readily detected in cells and with drug concentration increasing, more and more DOX was released and distributed in the whole cells. Moreover, 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), a fluorochrome used to label cell nucleus, was also employed to investigate the drug distribution. It was found that most DOX was concentrated around the nucleus, confirming that DOX mostly acted on DNA by breaking down DNA strand and finally caused cell death.

Encouraged by the excellent performance of MnO2-PDA-FA + CQDs + DOX, we then studied the cancer therapeutic effects. As a novel nanocarrier, toxicity is the top factor to be considered and cell viability study of MnO2 and MnO2-PDA-FA indicated that both samples showed very low toxicity even under high concentrations, and after PDA-FA modification, toxicity of MnO2 was further lowered and the cell viability was above 80% even under a high concentration of 50 μg/mL (Fig. 8 a). A comparison between HeLa and A549 cells treated by MnO2-PDA-FA + CQDs + DOX showed that under the same conditions, HeLa cells had relatively lower cell viability and this indicated that the constructed nanocarrier possessed special selectivity toward cancer cells with FA receptors (Fig. 8b), which agreed well with flow cytometry experiment results in Fig. 6. Then a more detailed cancer therapeutic study was carried out. As showed in Fig. 8c, for the three samples, with drug concentrations increasing, the cell viability gradually decreased, showing typical concentration-dependent behavior. Meanwhile, it was found that cancer cell inhibition effect of MnO2-PDA + CQDs + DOX was higher than pure DOX (Fig. 8d), and this might result from the facts that besides the chemotherapy effect, the reactive oxygen species of MnO2-PDA, which was induced by changing the balance of cell oxidation-reduction state and exerted oxidative pressure on cells [50], also contributed to the better effective therapy. Accordingly, it was no doubt that when the surface of MnO2-PDA was further modified by FA, much higher cell-uptake behavior was obtained by HeLa cells and thus, MnO2-PDA-FA + CQDs + DOX possessed the best cell inhibition effect. Similar results were also obtained when cancer cells were treated by 48 h.

In summary, we introduced a novel strategy to construct a multifunctional double-FRET triggered and fluorescence-switchable MnO2-PDA-FA + CQDs + DOX system. Highly fluorescent N-enriched CQDs were synthesized and then served as an effective fluorescence-switch for DOX by using the FRET mechanism between them. The loading amount of DOX onto CQDs could be well tuned and monitored by the fluorescence and solution-color changes, rendering facile and fast detection of drug loading process under naked eyes instead of using UV or fluorescence instrument for the first time. Then, MnO2 nanoparticles with high surface area were synthesized and further modified by PDA and FA. The chemical composition, morphology, and properties were systematacially studied, and experimental data revealed that MnO2-PDA-FA had good dispersibility in various media, biocompatibility and special selectivity toward cancer cells. Moreover, it was further employed as the other effective fluorescence-switch for CQDs + DOX, whose fluorescence could be totally quenched when loaded onto MnO2-PDA-FA, and responsively recovered due to the degradation of MnO2 in cancer cells with high GSH contents. Most importantly, the novel nanocarrier showed much better cancer therapeutic effect compared with using pure anticancer drugs under the same conditions, allowing more accurately drug delivery and more concentrated drug release that was effectively responsive to cancer intracellular conditions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation (ZR2018QB002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51905304, 21671118, 31901188), Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (J16LA03) of Shandong, and the Taishan Scholars. We also thanks ths financial support from National Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship (201910446027).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2019.107893.
